artifacts.content:
Hi Jas,

I’m hiding in the car outside Vons for ten minutes of quiet before tackling the dining room again, so of course I’m emailing you instead of actually decluttering.

I keep thinking about that happy hour at Stone Brewing in Liberty Station back in June 2023 when you first told me about Sue. At the time it sounded intense — panic attacks, six-week leave from LumaHealth, Sharp Mesa Vista, all of that — and I remember thinking, “wow, that’s rough, but I’m fine.” Fast forward to this past spring when my primary care at UC San Diego Health was talking about bumping my SSRI dose again and suggesting I look into coaching, and suddenly I didn’t feel so “fine” anymore.

The thing that finally pushed me over the edge was that Zoom with Brian in early March 2024. He framed it as “informal feedback,” but missing those two internal deadlines and almost sending the wrong build to that fintech client… yikes. When your manager at Coastal Nexus is gently asking whether you’ve considered an ADHD evaluation or an executive coach because your home office looks like a storage unit on camera, it’s probably time to stop pretending the piles are “temporary.”

So I dug up the notes from the January 2024 webinar you’d forwarded, re-watched Sue’s decluttering/time-blocking thing you sent in May 2023, and finally booked the Success Strategy Session. It weirdly helped to remember how your therapist at Sharp Mesa Vista had you doing routines and decluttering as part of going back to work.

Anyway, that’s my ramble from the Vons parking lot. How are things at LumaHealth? Still surviving sprint reviews?

Love,
Rebecca

metadata.subject: Catching you up from the Vons parking lot

artifacts.content:
Mari ❤️

I’m taking five between standups and wanted to say thank you again for forwarding that Rady EAP email back in February 2024. I honestly brushed it off at first — “compassion fatigue” sounded like something for you pediatric nurses after the winter 2023 RSV madness, not for someone who sits in front of Jira boards all day.

But the psychologist from the employee assistance program was right about how chronic disorganization at home makes everything heavier. By the time you came home from that mandatory wellness in-service at Rady Children’s and told me a bunch of nurses had ended up on short‑term disability after the RSV surge, I was already waking up at 3 a.m. thinking about my inbox and the piles on the dining table.

Then in October 2024 when Ana Gutierrez shared Sue’s webinar through your internal staff email — the one about time‑blocking and tracking all her court dates and supervised visits at the Family Visitation Center on El Cajon Blvd while working her 36‑hour/week schedule — it really clicked. If she can juggle custody hearings and two kids with those systems, I can probably manage sprint planning and Sofia’s band concerts.

So between that, Jasmine’s stories about her therapist at Sharp Mesa Vista having her declutter before going back to LumaHealth, and my own near‑meltdown after that early March 2024 Zoom with Brian, I finally admitted I’m edging into my own kind of burnout. Booking the Success Strategy Session felt less dramatic after hearing all of that.

Anyway, you’re on break soon — want me to start rice and pull the chicken out to defrost so you don’t walk into chaos on top of chaos?

Besos,
R

Subject: Sunday + Abuela + my latest “wake-up call”

Mami,

Just wanted to confirm we’re still on for lunch in National City this Sunday. I’ll bring the pan dulce from that place on 8th you like.

I need to confess something a little embarrassing before you see it yourself. The dining table here in Clairemont has turned into a baby version of Abuela Lucy’s condo in 2019. Last week I found three separate bills buried under a pile of Sofia’s school papers and old project notes. By the time I paid them, I’d racked up $180 in late fees and overdraft charges. When I called you to vent, your reminder about how Abuela stopped opening her mail when the dementia got worse — and how the condo in National City filled with unpaid notices — really shook me.

It’s not that I think I’m heading down the same path, but I can see how ignoring the clutter is a slippery slope. Between working remotely for Coastal Nexus, the PMP coursework through UCSD Extension, and trying to be present for Mari and Sofia, I’ve been pretending I can just “power through” the mess.

After I hung up with you, I re‑watched that decluttering webinar Jasmine had sent me back in May 2023 — the one from the coach who helped her go back to work after those panic attacks and the six‑week leave from LumaHealth and Sharp Mesa Vista. I finally booked a Success Strategy Session with her in late August 2023. I’m hoping she can help me get a handle on the paper piles before they turn into something scarier.

We can talk more Sunday. Don’t worry, I’m okay — just finally taking this seriously.

Te quiero,
Rebecca

Title: Why I’m doing this (for when I want to quit halfway)

Date: 2024-03-05

I keep wanting to bail on the whole “get organized” thing, so I’m writing this to myself.

1. Work reality check

Early March 2024: Zoom with Brian (Coastal Nexus). “Informal” warning about missing two internal deadlines and almost sending the wrong build to the client. He literally suggested I consider an ADHD evaluation or working with an executive coach because my home setup looks so chaotic on camera. I felt called out and also… he’s not wrong. If I want to grow from Senior Project Coordinator into a PM, I can’t keep winging it.

2. Health + anxiety

This past spring my primary care at UC San Diego Health talked about increasing my SSRI again because my anxiety has been spiking. I don’t want to just medicate my way through a life that’s structurally overwhelming. Jasmine’s story keeps echoing — panic attacks so bad she had to take a six‑week leave from LumaHealth, working with that therapist at Sharp Mesa Vista who had her decluttering and building routines before going back. I’m not there, but I can see the slope.

3. Money + Abuela

Late August 2023: $180 in late fees/overdrafts because I lost three bills in the dining room piles. Mami reminding me that in 2019, when Abuela Lucy’s dementia worsened, the first sign was all the unopened mail in her National City condo. That scared me enough to re‑watch the May 2023 decluttering webinar Jasmine sent and finally book the Success Strategy Session in August.

Remember: this is about feeling in control again, not about having a Pinterest house.

metadata.title: Why I’m doing this (for when I want to quit halfway)

artifacts.content:
Event: Success Strategy Session with Sue (virtual)

Date: Thursday, 2023-08-31
Time: 4:00–5:30 p.m.
Location: Home – dining room (clear half the table!)
Attendees: Rebecca Martinez, Sue (coach)

Notes / prep:

– Physical clutter to mention:
  • Dining room = office since early 2020. Dual monitors, piles of project notes, Sofia’s school papers, unopened mail.
  • Two bookshelves jammed with binders from old roles, UCSD Extension PMP printouts, training manuals I never touch.
  • Three cardboard boxes in the corner from the move three years ago, still full of Abuela Lucia’s recipe books, handwritten notes, and random keepsakes from the National City condo.
  • Sideboard stacked with “to deal with” papers, including the three bills I lost track of in late August 2023 that cost me $180 in late fees and overdraft charges.

– Digital clutter:
  • Thousands of unread emails across work + personal.
  • Relying on scattered sticky notes and my memory instead of a trusted system.

– Emotional context:
  • Mami’s reminder about Abuela’s unopened mail being the first sign her dementia was getting worse back in 2019.
  • Jasmine’s story about panic attacks, six‑week leave from LumaHealth, and how her therapist at Sharp Mesa Vista used decluttering and routines to get her back to work.
  • Want to avoid getting to the point where my primary care at UC San Diego Health is just upping my SSRI every year.

Goal for session: Leave with a simple starting plan for the dining room/office and 2–3 daily/weekly habits I can actually stick to.

Subject: Quick update before your shift

Hey Mari,

You’re probably grabbing coffee before nights at Rady, but I wanted to send a quick update while it’s quiet here.

I just finished re‑watching that October 2024 webinar Ana Gutierrez shared through your Rady Children’s internal staff email — the one where she talked about keeping track of all the court dates and supervised visits at the Family Visitation Center on El Cajon Blvd while still doing her 36‑hour/week social work schedule. Hearing her describe color‑coding the visitation schedule for her two kids next to her patient caseload made me weirdly hopeful. If she can manage that, there’s hope for my mix of sprint planning and PTA emails.

It also tied together with everything else that’s been nudging me: Jasmine’s experience with panic attacks and the six‑week leave from LumaHealth, her therapist at Sharp Mesa Vista making decluttering and routines part of getting her back to work; your wellness in‑service in February 2024 where the Rady EAP psychologist talked about chronic disorganization at home making compassion fatigue worse and mentioned this same coach by name; and then Brian’s informal warning in early March 2024 about my two missed internal deadlines and the almost‑disaster with the wrong build.

So I went ahead and booked a follow‑up package with Sue after my original Success Strategy Session last August. I want to get the dining room under control before Sofia’s band friends come over next month so we don’t have to shove my entire “office” onto the couch again.

I’ll start rice around 5 so it’s ready for you if you get a real dinner break tomorrow.

Love you,
R

Title: Big-picture goals for the next 6 months

Date: 2025-01-10
Location: Notion – Personal HQ

Career
– Continue to settle into Senior Project Coordinator role at Coastal Nexus (promotion Oct 2025).
– Aim for formal Project Manager path by mid‑2026.
– To do that, I need to avoid repeats of early March 2024 (Zoom with Brian, two missed internal deadlines, almost sending wrong build). Systems, not heroics.

Home + clutter
– Transform dining room from “pandemic emergency office” into flexible workspace/dining area.
– Finish going through the three boxes of Abuela Lucia’s things from the National City condo: keep recipe books and a small selection of handwritten notes, let go of random kitchen gadgets.
– Keep paper flow under control so there’s no repeat of late August 2023 ($180 in late fees/overdrafts from three lost bills).

Mental health
– Keep SSRI dose stable if possible; focus on routines. Remember: my primary care at UC San Diego Health suggested coaching instead of just increasing meds again.
– Use what I learned from Sue’s Success Strategy Session (booked Aug 31, 2023 after re‑watching Jasmine’s May 2023 decluttering webinar) and the follow‑ups I scheduled after hearing about the Rady EAP talk in Feb 2024 and Ana’s October 2024 webinar.
– Don’t wait until I’m where Jasmine was — panic attacks, six‑week leave from LumaHealth, therapist at Sharp Mesa Vista rebuilding her life from the ground up.

Family
– More Sunday lunches with Mami and Papi in National City.
– One dedicated “Sofia night” per week that is NOT at the cluttered dining table (walk, movie, or board game).
– Support Mari through her own compassion fatigue after the winter RSV and flu seasons at Rady.

metadata.title: Big-picture goals for the next 6 months
